Video Title: You don't need 47 random colors. You need one proper scale.  HSL method: → Start with base at 500 e.g. hsl(230, 77%, 50%) → Define edges: 900 (darkest) & 100 (lightest) → Keep same hue, adjust lightness & saturation → Fill the gaps Don't trust numbers  check contrast in real UI. If it feels off, rotate the hue slightly. HSL = structure Your eyes = final decision #colortheory #uiuxdesign #designtips #graphicdesign    Day 3 of the #squibbchallenge @Simon Squibb

Linear scales fail. Non-linear scales work. Two popular methods: → Modular (e.g. ×1.25) → Fibonacci (13→21→34) Growing gaps create hierarchy. Tools: typescale.com | modularscale.com #typography #uiuxdesign #designtips #graphicdesign   Day 4 of #squibbchallenge

Stop the color chaos! Use the 60-30-10 Rule for pro designs. It’s the visual secret behind Spotify & Netflix. ~ 60% Neutral  ~ 30% Secondary  ~ 10% Accent Save this for your next project! What is your favorite design principle? #squibbchallenge #uiux #colortheory #designrules #designsystem   Day 1, @Simon Squibb

Blank screens feel slow because they give no feedback. Skeletons provide structure, reduce uncertainty and can feel up to ~50% faster at the same load time. Users form judgments within seconds. Loading states must match duration and context. Perceived performance is a design decision. Day 22 of #squibbchallenge  #uidesign #perceivedperformance #loadingstates #interactiondesign

Pure Black (#/000000) is Killing Your Design!  On screens, it creates harsh contrast and strains the eyes. That's why big players like Spotify and Netflix use Near Black instead. The Trade-Off: Choose Impact (Apple) or Comfort (Netflix)? Near Black is calmer, more refined, and better for UX. #uiuxdesign  #designtips  #colortheory #graphicdesign   Day 2 of the #squibbchallenge

Bad spacing kills readability.  Line height is the vertical space between lines of text and getting it right changes everything. Here's a quick guideline: - Body text? 1.5–1.6 - Headings? 1.2 - Small print? 1.6–1.7 How do you handle line height? Day 5 of #squibbchallenge  #lineheight #typography #graphicdesign #uidesign

Border radius needs rules. Pick a base unit. Scale proportionally. Inner radius = outer radius - padding. Small components = tight radius. Document it. Use it everywhere. Day 7 of #squibbchallenge  #borderradius #designsystems #uidesign #graphicdesign

Grids are the invisible bones of design. Stop guessing. Start structuring. Manuscript Grid = One column. Pure focus. (Articles, books - uninterrupted text flow) Column Grid = Vertical rhythm. (Newspapers, blogs - content in clean lanes) Modular Grid = Rows + columns. (Magazines, dashboards - complex interfaces) Hierarchical Grid = Break rules with intention. (Hero sections, landing pages - guide attention) Design is not decoration. It's controlled order. Day 9 of #squibbchallenge #gridsystems #uidesign #uxdesign #designprinciples

One primary button per screen. That's the rule. Most interfaces break this. Multiple CTAs compete for attention.  Result? Decision paralysis. Primary commits. Secondary offers choice. Placement = information. Put your CTA where users finish their task, not floating in a corner. Buttons need 5 states: Normal. Hover. Focus. Active. Disabled. Links are not buttons. Don't make them think. How many button states do you design?  Day 30 of #squibbchallenge #uidesign #uxdesign #productdesign #designsystems

Your static progress bars create loading anxiety during active processes. Animation signals active loading, static bars indicate completion or paused state. The goal-gradient effect proves motivation peaks at 80% as users perceive the remaining distance shrinking rapidly. Do you use determinate or indeterminate loaders for unknown durations? #uidesigntips #progressbar #uxdesign #loadingstate #interfacedesign

Jakob's Law: Users spend 95% of their time on other sites. Familiar patterns = instant comfort. Creative navigation = cognitive overload. Your logo goes top-left. Search goes top-right. Because that's where users already look. Don't innovate the interface. Innovate the value. Good design feels invisible. What's one pattern you'll never break? Day 13 of #squibbchallenge  #jakobslaw #uxdesign #uidesign #usability

Why do most websites follow the same layout?  F-Pattern. One of three major scan patterns. Eye-tracking reveals: → F-Pattern: Content-heavy pages (blogs, news) → Z-Pattern: Landing pages, simple layouts → Layer Cake: Scanning between headers When F-Pattern applies: • Text-heavy content • Multiple sections • Left-to-right reading cultures The Rules: • Top-left = Prime attention • First words of lines = Critical • Users scan, don't read The Reality: Wrong pattern for your content type = ignored content. Match your layout to user behavior.  Day 14 of #squibbchallenge  #fpattern #eyetracking #uxpatterns #designengineering

Inconsistent card layouts destroy comparison speed in dashboards and feeds. Users scan top-to-bottom expecting visual anchors first and actions last, reversing this order breaks learned patterns. Fitts Law proves larger click targets reduce interaction cost, which is why the entire card should be clickable, not just a tiny CTA button buried inside. Do you make entire cards clickable or only buttons inside? #uidesigntips #carddesign #uxdesign #interfacedesign #designsystem

One spacing scale. Two layers. 4 · 8 · 12 · 16 · 20 · 24 · 32 · 40 · 48 4pt values handle precision: icons, typography baselines, and internal component spacing. 8pt values create rhythm: layout spacing, padding, margins, and component sizing. Modern design systems don’t choose between 4pt or 8pt. They assign responsibility. Same numbers. Clear rules. #DesignSystems #SpacingSystem #8ptGrid #4ptGrid #UIDesign

Font weight builds hierarchy. Not decoration. Nine weights. 100 to 900. Each serves a purpose. The breakdown: Thin (100-200) → Headlines only. Fails at small sizes. Light (300) → Elegance for large type. Unreadable below 16px. Regular (400) → Body text standard. Optimal readability. Medium (500-600) → Subtle emphasis. UI elements. Navigation. Bold (700) → Headings. CTAs. Clear focus points. Black (800-900) → Maximum impact. Hero sections only. The principle: Heavier weight = higher importance. Lighter weight = less visual priority. The mistake: Mixing too many weights (visual chaos) or using thin for body copy (accessibility fail). Stop guessing weight. Start matching purpose. What's your go-to font weight combo? Day 11 of #squibbchallenge   #fontweight #typography #uidesign #uxdesign

Your lists fail because they lack structure.  Visual anchors on the left are essential for scanning, as that is where 80% of user attention is naturally focused. To minimize interaction time, every row must be a full-width target with a height of ≥ 44px. Dropping below this threshold isn't just a layout issue, it triples error rates for your users. Eliminate visual noise by choosing one structural method: spacing OR lines. Using both creates redundant clutter that slows down information processing. #uidesign #uxdesign #designsystem #fittslaw #graphicdesign

Icons look misaligned when geometrically centered next to text. Text uses baseline, ascenders, descenders, and cap height, icons use bounding boxes. Mathematical centering fails visually. A vertical offset of ~0.05 to ~0.2em creates optical balance. Flexbox align-items center is your starting point, not your solution. Visual perception beats geometric precision. #uidesigntips #typography #icondesign #uxdesign #design

List layouts create cognitive overload. Identical rows without visual structure force sequential scanning, that's extraneous load. Cards chunk information using Gestalt Closure, dual coding activates two memory channels, and larger touch targets reduce error rates by 40%. High information density requires low cognitive friction. Which UI pattern overloads you the most? Endless lists, cluttered dashboards, or hidden navigation? #cognitiveload #uxdesign #uidesigntips #mobileui #design

Center bias breakdown: Your brain fixates center first, hardwired biology that even infants show. Nearly all first fixations land center. Never crowd the middle. One anchor only creates clarity. Multiple elements competing destroy the effect instantly. Edges for utility, center for conversion. Color psychology breakdown: Red (urgency + sales), Blue (trust + finance), Yellow (attention + warnings), Green (nature + bio). Cultural context matters, red means danger in the West, luck in China.
